question: A distribution channel is:
options: The specific shipping method used to send products to customers

The set of intermediaries or direct pathways through which a product moves from producer to the final consumer — including wholesalers, distributors, retailers, e-commerce platforms, and direct sales forces; also called the marketing channel or trade channel
The company's internal logistics and warehouse network
The television and radio channels used to advertise products
explanation: Distribution channels: a product can reach a customer through multiple paths. A Coca-Cola bottle: Coca-Cola (producer) → bottling partner → regional distributor → grocery retailer → consumer (4 levels). Or: Coca-Cola → restaurant/fountain account (2 levels). Or: Coca-Cola.com direct order (1 level). Channel design is strategic: it determines reach (how many customers can you access?), cost (what margins do intermediaries take?), control (who owns the customer relationship?), and speed to market. Channel strategy is one of the four Ps (Place/Distribution) and critically shapes competitive position.

question: Channel conflict occurs when:
options: Multiple distribution channels compete for the same customers in ways that harm the interests of channel members — e.g., a manufacturer selling directly to consumers (DTC) at the same time it sells through retailers who now compete with the manufacturer for the same customers; retailers feel undermined, may deprioritize the manufacturer's products, and may retaliate by promoting competing brands
Two transportation companies compete for the same shipping contract
A product receives negative reviews on multiple retail platforms
Different marketing messages appear in different distribution channels
explanation: Channel conflict example: Nike launched Nike.com direct-to-consumer sales while continuing to sell through Foot Locker, Dick's Sporting Goods, and other retailers. Foot Locker complained that Nike was competing with them for the same consumers. Nike responded by deprioritizing Foot Locker (less product allocation) and investing more in Nike Direct — a deliberate decision to accept channel conflict in favor of DTC economics (higher margin, better customer data). Managing channel conflict: transparent channel pricing policies, differentiated product lines by channel (exclusive styles for DTC, different styles for retailers), or clear territory/customer segment division.

question: An intensive distribution strategy places products in:
options: Only the highest-end retail locations to maintain brand exclusivity

As many retail locations as possible to maximize product availability and convenience — appropriate for frequently purchased, low-involvement products (Coca-Cola, Tide, chewing gum) where consumers don't seek out specific stores and purchase decisions are made based on immediate availability
Only stores that have agreed to exclusive distribution agreements
Only stores located in major metropolitan areas
explanation: Distribution intensity strategies: (1) Intensive: maximize availability, every possible outlet; appropriate for: convenience goods, FMCG, commodities — Coca-Cola in 200+ countries, 3M+ outlets; consumers buy whichever brand is available. (2) Selective: choose specific retail partners; appropriate for: shopping goods, electronics (Apple in Best Buy and Apple Stores, not in every gas station); moderate search behavior, some brand preference. (3) Exclusive: single retailer per territory; appropriate for: specialty goods, luxury (Rolex in authorized jewelers only), high-service products; consumers seek out specific brands. The choice determines: market reach vs. brand control.

question: What is the difference between a 'push' and 'pull' distribution strategy?
options: Push and pull refer to the direction of physical product flow in the supply chain

Push: the manufacturer uses trade incentives (discounts, promotions, advertising allowances) to push product into the distribution channel and get retailers/distributors to stock and promote it — the manufacturer is pushing product toward the consumer through intermediaries. Pull: the manufacturer uses consumer advertising to create demand that 'pulls' product through the channel — consumers go to retailers and ask for the product, forcing retailers to stock it to meet demand; most companies use a combination, but the mix determines who drives channel decisions
Push is always more effective than pull for consumer goods
Pull strategy only applies to e-commerce distribution channels
explanation: Push vs. pull distribution: Procter & Gamble uses both — trade promotions (slotting fees, case discounts, co-op advertising) push products into Walmart/Target shelves; consumer advertising for Tide creates pull demand that makes retailers want to stock Tide regardless of trade promotions. A small startup without consumer brand awareness must use push (trade promotions to get shelf space) because there's no pull demand yet. A mega-brand like Coca-Cola uses pull — its consumer demand is so strong that retailers cannot choose to not stock it. The balance between push and pull investments is a fundamental channel strategy decision.

question: Apple uses both an exclusive retail channel (Apple Stores) and sells through Best Buy, carrier stores (AT&T, Verizon), and Amazon. Why does Apple use multiple channel types simultaneously?
options: Apple uses multiple channels because it cannot control which channels sell its products

Multi-channel strategy serving different purchase occasions and customer segments: Apple Store (exclusive, premium experience): provides the full brand experience, best for customers buying their first Apple product or needing education about a complex product (Mac); provides Apple full margin (no retailer cut) and complete customer data. Best Buy/carrier stores: provides access to customers who are physically in electronics stores or making mobile carrier decisions; these customers may not go to an Apple Store first. Amazon: online convenience for repurchase, accessories, and customers who already know what they want. Each channel serves a different purchase journey; using all three channels maximizes total addressable market reach; the trade-off: channel conflict management (ensuring Best Buy doesn't feel undermined by Apple Direct)
Multi-channel distribution always reduces brand consistency
Apple should only sell through Apple Stores to maintain full brand control
explanation: Apple's multi-channel strategy: Apple Stores (530+ worldwide) contribute ~20% of Apple's direct revenue and disproportionate brand value — the store IS the brand experience. But Apple cannot reach every consumer through its own stores (no Apple Store in rural Iowa; no Apple Store in most of India). The carrier channel is critical for iPhone: most US consumers purchase iPhones through their mobile carrier when upgrading plans — Apple must be available where customers are making that decision. Amazon: iPhone accessories, Apple Watch bands, AppleCare — repurchase and accessories are convenience-driven. The channel portfolio maximizes reach while Apple maintains price consistency (same iPhone price everywhere) to prevent channel conflict on pricing.

question: A wine brand sells exclusively through exclusive restaurants (prestige channel). A private equity buyer wants to expand distribution to grocery stores to grow revenue 10x. What are the risks of this channel expansion?
options: Channel expansion to grocery stores has no risks since it increases revenue

Channel expansion risks for prestige brands: (1) Brand equity dilution — a wine sold exclusively through Michelin-starred restaurants has a 'scarcity' and 'prestige' association; seeing the same bottle at Safeway destroys the exclusivity narrative; (2) Price pressure — grocery retail chains expect 40%+ margin, requiring lower wholesale prices that compress margins or require price increases; (3) Alienating existing channel — restaurants that feature the wine exclusively may drop it when it becomes 'widely available'; (4) Consumer perception shift — research shows that consumers rate the same wine lower in quality when they see it in a grocery store vs. a fine restaurant; the correct approach: create a separate label/brand for grocery distribution to protect the prestige channel while accessing mass market revenue
Expanding to grocery stores never dilutes brand equity
Prestige wine brands always benefit from wider distribution
explanation: Channel extension and brand equity tension: the challenge for prestige brands moving to mass distribution. Case study: Mondavi Woodbridge (Robert Mondavi Winery) — Mondavi created the Woodbridge label specifically for grocery distribution, protecting the 'Robert Mondavi Reserve' prestige brand. The brands shared winemaking expertise but were clearly separate in positioning, packaging, and pricing. This 'flanker brand' strategy is how prestige companies access mass markets without destroying their anchor brand. Alternatives: limited-edition grocery-exclusive packaging; select premium grocery retailers only (Whole Foods, not Walmart); or online DTC direct at premium prices that replicates the exclusivity context.

question: Amazon's marketplace allows third-party sellers to list and sell products through Amazon.com. Amazon takes 15–40% commission. A brand selling on Amazon through its own seller account discovers a third-party seller offering its products at 40% below MSRP. How should the brand respond?
options: Accept the unauthorized seller's discounting since Amazon controls pricing policy

Multi-pronged channel management response: (1) Identify the unauthorized seller source: how are they getting inventory below MSRP? (diverter buying surplus from authorized retailers? gray market imports? direct factory leakage?); (2) Enforce Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policies — require all authorized sellers to adhere to MAP; report violations to Amazon through its Seller Performance team; (3) Amazon Brand Registry + 'Project Zero' — enrolled brands can automatically remove counterfeit or unauthorized listings; (4) Address the supply chain leak — if authorized retailers are diverting excess inventory to unauthorized sellers, restrict supply to those retailers; (5) Consider 'authorized seller' exclusivity programs that give designated sellers advantages in exchange for pricing compliance; the 40% discount is harmful to: brand equity (devalues the product), authorized retailers (can't compete), and the brand's Amazon economics (Amazon may match the lower price, reducing the brand's margin)
Lower prices on Amazon always benefit the brand through increased volume
Brands cannot control pricing by third-party sellers on Amazon's marketplace
explanation: Amazon unauthorized seller problem: 'gray market' and 'diversion' are major channel management issues for consumer brands. Diversion: authorized retailers buy excess inventory (especially at end-of-season clearance) and resell to unauthorized Amazon sellers at below-MSRP prices. This undermines authorized channel partners, erodes brand value, and creates customer confusion (is this authentic?). The brand's toolkit: (1) MAP policies and enforcement; (2) Amazon Brand Registry (authenticates the brand, gives enforcement tools); (3) Serialized units that can be tracked back to the authorized retailer that diverted them; (4) Structural: don't over-supply authorized retailers (excess inventory creates the diversion opportunity). Companies like Nike and Apple maintain tight supply to authorized channels precisely to prevent gray market diversion.

question: A consumer goods startup sells through a network of independent retailers who set their own prices. The startup's brand is growing and it wants to ensure consistent retail pricing. Can the startup legally require all retailers to charge the same price?
options: No — minimum price requirements are always illegal per se violations of antitrust law

In the US, vertical minimum price fixing (Resale Price Maintenance, or RPM) was ruled per se illegal in Dr. Miles Medical (1911) but was reversed by Leegin Creative Leather Products v. PSKS (2007) — RPM is now judged under the 'rule of reason' (allowed if pro-competitive effects outweigh anti-competitive harms); legal alternatives: (1) Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) — restrict how low the price can be advertised, not the actual selling price; (2) Suggested Retail Price (SRP/MSRP) — suggest but don't mandate; (3) Consignment pricing (manufacturer retains title, sets price); (4) Selective distribution — only sell to retailers who agree to pricing guidelines; the brand must work with legal counsel because RPM legality varies by country (many countries still treat it as per se illegal)
Price coordination between any manufacturer and retailer is always illegal
Brands have no tools to influence retail pricing for their products
explanation: Channel pricing control and antitrust: RPM legality is a complex area that requires case-by-case analysis. In practice: MAP policies are widely used by brands (Apple, Nike, Lego, Coach) to maintain consistent pricing perception. MAP says 'you can sell at any price, but you cannot advertise below $X.' This is legally defensible because it restricts advertising, not actual transaction price. The business rationale for MAP: prevents 'showrooming' (consumers visit store to see product, then buy online at lowest price); maintains retailer margin (ensures all retailers can earn enough to invest in display and staff training); and prevents price-based brand commoditization. MAP violation enforcement: warn first, then cut off supply to non-compliant retailers.

question: Costco sells a limited assortment (4,000 SKUs vs. 30,000+ at a typical grocery store) of high-volume items at near-wholesale prices. What channel and distribution model does this represent, and why does it work?
options: Costco uses the same distribution model as traditional grocery stores with a different name

Costco's membership warehouse model is a distinct channel design: (1) Limited SKU = warehouse efficiency (pallets directly on sales floor, no repackaging, minimal handling labor); (2) Membership fee ($65–130/year) = the actual profit engine (membership fees represent ~75% of Costco's operating profit, allowing near-zero product margins); (3) High-volume buying = better supplier terms (Costco negotiates from strength with limited SKU exclusivity commitments); (4) Members buy in bulk = higher average basket size ($140 vs. $50 at traditional grocery); (5) No advertising = cost savings reinvested in lower prices; this model only works because the membership creates selection bias (Costco members have higher income and willingness to buy in bulk) and the membership fee pre-commits buyers to justify their membership through purchase behavior
Costco's low prices are possible only because it sells lower-quality products
Costco's business model is only viable due to government subsidies for bulk retailers
explanation: Costco's channel model economics: the 'membership warehouse' is a fundamentally different channel architecture. Gross margin on merchandise: ~11–12% (vs. 25–35% at traditional grocery). Operating profit on merchandise: ~3% (near zero). Membership fee revenue: $4.5B/year (2023) = ~80% of total operating income. The model works because: (1) Membership selection bias: only price-conscious, bulk-buying consumers pay the annual fee; (2) Limited assortment drives volume per SKU (Costco sells one toilet paper brand in huge volume vs. 30 options at grocery — better supplier pricing from concentration); (3) Warehouse operations (pallets as displays) = dramatically lower labor cost per unit sold. The implication: Costco sells products nearly at cost, profits entirely from membership — a completely different business model than every other retailer.

question: A B2B industrial equipment manufacturer currently sells through a network of regional distributors (100+ distributors who carry stock, provide service, and cover local markets). The manufacturer wants to launch a direct e-commerce channel. What channel management issues must be addressed?
options: There are no channel management issues since e-commerce is a separate channel

Direct channel launch would create serious channel conflict with existing distributors: (1) Direct price competition — if the manufacturer sells direct at distributor list price, distributors lose sales; if at lower price, distributors are undercut by their own supplier; (2) Distributor relationship damage — distributors have invested in inventory, service capability, and customer relationships; a direct channel undermines their investment and may cause them to prioritize competing brands; (3) Service complexity — industrial equipment requires installation, training, and parts support; the manufacturer may lack this capability directly; Constructive resolution options: (a) DTC only for products distributors don't carry (new products, spare parts); (b) Lead generation model (DTC site captures inquiries, routes to nearest distributor); (c) Differentiated assortment (new product lines exclusively through DTC, existing lines exclusively through distribution); (d) Shared revenue model (distributor earns commission on DTC sales in their territory)
Distributors have no legitimate claim to prevent manufacturers from selling directly
E-commerce channels always replace distributor channels in B2B markets
explanation: B2B channel conflict management is one of the most complex challenges in distribution strategy. Industrial distributors have built their businesses around representing specific manufacturers — they've hired service technicians, built parts inventories, and developed customer relationships. When a manufacturer launches direct, distributors face: (1) Revenue cannibalization; (2) Margin pressure if manufacturer prices direct at lower levels; (3) Strategic uncertainty about the long-term value of their partnership. The manufacturer's dilemma: distributors provide local market access, service capability, and inventory at low fixed cost — capabilities that are expensive to replicate directly. Destroying the distributor network to go direct may gain margins but lose market reach, local service quality, and create a 3-year capability gap in building direct service operations.
